critic_raw:
Overall, this is a methodologically mature design. The temporal split is correctly motivated, preprocessing leakage is explicitly blocked, the ablation matrix is well-specified, and AUPRC is the right primary metric for this imbalance regime. However, I see three genuine issues worth raising.

**Issue 1: Sender reputation and graph features are computed from 'historical sending patterns' — but 'historical' is ambiguous relative to the test window.**

The methodology states reputation scores and graph edges are derived from communications 'prior to the email send time' and that the reputation normalizer is 'fit on training data only.' This partially addresses leakage, but there is a structural problem: the graph and reputation signals computed for test-set emails (months 16–18) necessarily incorporate communications from months 1–15, including the validation period. That is fine for the graph edges themselves (they reflect actual prior communication history), but the *statistics used to encode or normalize* those features — e.g., the distribution of reputation scores, the embedding space for sender/recipient nodes — are fixed from months 1–12. 

The problem is that sender nodes that were low-activity or unseen during training will have poorly calibrated embeddings or reputation estimates when they become high-volume senders by month 16. More critically, attackers who 'warm up' sender accounts during months 13–15 (a known BEC tactic) will appear legitimate by the test window because their pre-send-time history now includes months of benign traffic not seen during training. The reputation signal inverts: the warmup makes them look trusted. This is not a leakage problem — it is a systematic signal degradation that the design does not measure or acknowledge. The temporal degradation metric (months 16–17 vs. 18) will not isolate this because it does not stratify by sender account age or reputation trajectory.

Evidence that would resolve this: Report model performance stratified by whether the sender was active during training vs. appearing only in the validation/test window. If the graph/reputation-enriched model degrades more than the text-only baseline for novel senders, the reputation signal is a liability under distribution shift, not an asset.

**Issue 2: The baseline is under-specified in a way that likely inflates the transformer's apparent advantage.**

The text-only baseline uses TF-IDF/bag-of-words plus logistic regression or XGBoost. For phishing email detection, the most discriminative signals in this regime are not bag-of-words frequencies — they are URL patterns, HTML structure, header anomalies, and subject-line lexical markers (urgency phrases, impersonation tokens). TF-IDF over the full body will dilute these signals by weighting them against the full vocabulary. A principled baseline would at minimum include: (a) URL feature extraction, (b) header anomaly flags (SPF/DKIM/DMARC fail, display-name/from-domain mismatch), (c) character n-grams to capture obfuscation patterns. 

The methodology justifies the baseline as 'a principled text-only classifier,' but this framing is self-serving. The baseline receives *less information* than is available to a text-only system, because the design excludes headers from the baseline's feature set (they are mentioned in data collection but not in the baseline's feature list). Any advantage the transformer shows over this baseline is confounded with the baseline being artificially weak. The ablation in Section 7 partially addresses this by enriching the baseline with metadata, but the enriched baseline still excludes headers, and there is no ablation of a feature-engineered baseline that uses URL and header features.

Evidence: Add a header-feature-augmented baseline (SPF/DKIM/DMARC status, from/reply-to mismatch, URL domain reputation). If this baseline closes most of the gap, the transformer's contextual modeling is not doing meaningful work beyond what hand-crafted signals capture.

**Issue 3: Hyperparameter selection on months 13–15 may be biased toward threat patterns that are not representative of the test window.**

The validation set covers a fixed 3-month window. If a specific campaign or attack wave dominated months 13–15 (e.g., a seasonal phishing surge), the selected hyperparameters will be optimized for that campaign's signature. The test set (months 16–18) may contain a different distribution of campaigns. This is the standard 'one validation window' problem. For 3-month validation windows in a threat landscape, this is not a theoretical concern — known seasonal patterns (e.g., tax-season BEC, holiday gift-card phishing) create within-year distributional shifts that are correlated with calendar position.

The design acknowledges concept drift in the temporal degradation metric but does not acknowledge that the hyperparameter selection itself may be overfitted to a specific drift regime. The practical consequence is that validation AUPRC will be optimistically correlated with test AUPRC only if months 13–15 are representative of 16–18, which is not guaranteed and is not tested.

Evidence: Report validation AUPRC vs. test AUPRC for both models. A large gap (validation >> test) would indicate this problem. The methodology should specify what constitutes an acceptable gap and how they would interpret a large one.
all_issues_raised: Sender warmup attacks during the validation window degrade reputation/graph signals in the test window in a way the design does not measure or control for.
The text-only baseline excludes headers and URL features, making it artificially weak and inflating the transformer's apparent advantage.
Hyperparameter selection on a fixed 3-month validation window may overfit to campaign-specific drift patterns that do not generalize to the test window.
all_issues_adjudicated: Sender warmup attacks during the validation window degrade reputation/graph signals in the test window in a way the design does not measure or control for.
The text-only baseline excludes headers and URL features, making it artificially weak and inflating the transformer's apparent advantage.
Hyperparameter selection on a fixed 3-month validation window may overfit to campaign-specific drift patterns that do not generalize to the test window.
